critic_raw:
The design applies BERT to 'interaction logs' of course access frequency, forum participation, and assignment engagements — but BERT is a language model that expects natural language input, not structured behavioral logs. The design never clarifies how structured interaction data (e.g., 'user accessed module 3 fourteen times') is converted to BERT-compatible text input. If interaction logs are simply serialized as structured text, BERT's pre-trained positional embeddings and subword tokenization are misaligned with numerical frequency sequences. If interaction logs are converted to natural language descriptions, the conversion process introduces information compression that is not characterized. This is a prerequisite architectural assumption that determines whether the experiment is coherent.

Oversampling dropout instances in training without applying inverse probability weighting at evaluation or calibrating post-hoc means the model's output probabilities will be systematically inflated above the true dropout rate. AUPRC is less sensitive to this calibration shift than accuracy, but any threshold-based deployment decisions drawn from this model will be made at a probability scale that does not match the true population dropout rate.

The stratified random split by academic year cohort dropout distribution is particularly problematic for student dropout: dropout rates are cohort-dependent because course difficulty, instructor quality, and platform changes affect entire cohorts simultaneously. A student from cohort Year 1 who does not drop out may be systematically different from a non-dropout in Year 3 due to platform improvements, not individual characteristics. A random split that mixes cohorts in training and test treats between-cohort variation as noise rather than as the primary distribution shift of interest.

The BERT model uses both 'interaction text data and engineered metrics' — combining structured features with language model embeddings requires a fusion architecture (e.g., concatenation, cross-attention, gating) that is not described. The fusion approach is a major architectural choice that determines whether structured features dominate or are overpowered by the high-dimensional BERT representation.
all_issues_raised: BERT is applied to structured interaction logs (access frequencies, forum participation counts) rather than natural language text — the design does not specify how structured data is converted to BERT-compatible input, and if serialized as numerical sequences, BERT's pre-trained positional embeddings and subword tokenization are misaligned with the input format.

Oversampling dropout instances without post-hoc calibration correction produces systematically inflated output probabilities — any threshold-based early intervention system built on this model will be calibrated to an oversampled training distribution, not the true population dropout rate.

Stratified random split mixes student cohorts across training and test — dropout is cohort-dependent due to course and platform changes, so between-cohort variation is the primary distribution shift of interest and should be evaluated via cohort-based holdout, not averaged away by random splitting.

The fusion architecture combining BERT embeddings with engineered interaction metrics is unspecified — the choice of fusion method (concatenation, cross-attention, learned gating) is a major architectural decision that determines whether structured signals are preserved or overwhelmed by the high-dimensional language model representation.
all_issues_adjudicated: BERT is applied to structured interaction logs (access frequencies, forum participation counts) rather than natural language text — the design does not specify how structured data is converted to BERT-compatible input, and if serialized as numerical sequences, BERT's pre-trained positional embeddings and subword tokenization are misaligned with the input format.

Oversampling dropout instances without post-hoc calibration correction produces systematically inflated output probabilities — any threshold-based early intervention system built on this model will be calibrated to an oversampled training distribution, not the true population dropout rate.

Stratified random split mixes student cohorts across training and test — dropout is cohort-dependent due to course and platform changes, so between-cohort variation is the primary distribution shift of interest and should be evaluated via cohort-based holdout, not averaged away by random splitting.

The fusion architecture combining BERT embeddings with engineered interaction metrics is unspecified — the choice of fusion method (concatenation, cross-attention, learned gating) is a major architectural decision that determines whether structured signals are preserved or overwhelmed by the high-dimensional language model representation.
